([British](/wiki/British_English "British English"), [Indian](/wiki/Indian_English "Indian English"), [Pakistani](/wiki/Pakistani_English "Pakistani English"), and [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English")), **license plate** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")) or **licence plate** ([Canadian English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English")), is a [metal](/wiki/Metal "Metal") or [plastic](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic") plate attached to a [motor vehicle](/wiki/Motor_vehicle "Motor vehicle") or [trailer](/wiki/Trailer_(vehicle) "Trailer (vehicle)") for official identification purposes. All countries require registration plates for commercial road vehicles such as cars, trucks, and motorcycles, for hire. Whether they are required for other vehicles, such as bicycles, boats, or tractors, may vary by jurisdiction. The registration identifier is a numeric or alphanumeric ID that [uniquely identifies](/wiki/Unique_identifier "Unique identifier") the vehicle or vehicle owner within the issuing region's [vehicle register](/wiki/Vehicle_register "Vehicle register"). In some countries, the identifier is unique within the entire country, while in others it is unique within a state or province. Whether the identifier is associated with a vehicle or a person also varies by issuing agency. There are also [electronic license plates](/wiki/Electronic_license_plate "Electronic license plate").

Two  [Fiat 500s](/wiki/Fiat_500_(2007) "Fiat 500 (2007)"), which are operated in [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid") by the same company. Note that both vehicle registration plates finish in MYF. This is due to the company buying these [en masse](/wiki/En_masse "En masse").

A **vehicle registration plate**, also known as a **number plate** ([British](/wiki/British_English "British English"), [Indian](/wiki/Indian_English "Indian English"), [Pakistani](/wiki/Pakistani_English "Pakistani English"), and [Australian English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English")), **license plate** ([American English](/wiki/American_English "American English")) or **licence plate** ([Canadian English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English")), is a [metal](/wiki/Metal "Metal") or [plastic](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic") plate attached to a [motor vehicle](/wiki/Motor_vehicle "Motor vehicle") or [trailer](/wiki/Trailer_(vehicle) "Trailer (vehicle)") for official identification purposes. All countries require registration plates for commercial road vehicles such as cars, trucks, and motorcycles, for hire. Whether they are required for other vehicles, such as bicycles, boats, or tractors, may vary by jurisdiction. The registration identifier is a numeric or alphanumeric ID that [uniquely identifies](/wiki/Unique_identifier "Unique identifier") the vehicle or vehicle owner within the issuing region's [vehicle register](/wiki/Vehicle_register "Vehicle register"). In some countries, the identifier is unique within the entire country, while in others it is unique within a state or province. Whether the identifier is associated with a vehicle or a person also varies by issuing agency. There are also [electronic license plates](/wiki/Electronic_license_plate "Electronic license plate").

## Legal requirements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Legal requirements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/British_car_registration_plate_labels-UK.svg/250px-British_car_registration_plate_labels-UK.svg.png)](/wiki/File:British_car_registration_plate_labels-UK.svg)

A car registration plate from the United Kingdom. The "GB" or "UK" marks have been used in the United Kingdom in various years.[[1]](#cite_note-XI-B-19-1)

In Europe, most governments require a registration plate to be attached to both the front and rear of a vehicle,[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) although certain jurisdictions or vehicle types, such as [motorcycles](/wiki/Motorcycle "Motorcycle"), require only one plate, which is usually attached to the rear of the vehicle. Special vehicles, such as agricultural and construction equipment, might have the license plate attached to other parts of the vehicle. National databases relate this number to other information describing the vehicle, such as the make, [model](/wiki/Car_model "Car model"), [colour](/wiki/Car_colour "Car colour"), year of manufacture, [engine](/wiki/Engine "Engine") size, type of [fuel](/wiki/Fuel "Fuel") used, [mileage](/wiki/Odometer "Odometer") recorded (and other similar data in jurisdictions where vehicles are regularly [inspected for roadworthiness](/wiki/Vehicle_inspection "Vehicle inspection") every year or two), [vehicle identification number](/wiki/Vehicle_identification_number "Vehicle identification number") (chassis number), and the name and address of the vehicle's registered owner or keeper.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Letafors._Far_from_home_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Letafors._Far_from_home_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letafors._Far_from_home_-_panoramio.jpg)

Two Swedish snowmobiles with license plates attached to the side of the vehicles

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/2018_Victoria_registration_plate_2AX_8MH_The_Education_State.jpg/250px-2018_Victoria_registration_plate_2AX_8MH_The_Education_State.jpg)](/wiki/File:2018_Victoria_registration_plate_2AX_8MH_The_Education_State.jpg)

Some license plates such as this [Victorian](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia") one features holograms to deter numberplate cloning. [[4]](#cite_note-4)

In the vast majority of jurisdictions, the government holds a monopoly on the manufacturing of vehicle registration plates for that jurisdiction. Either a government agency or a private company with express contractual authorization from the government makes plates as needed, which are then mailed to, delivered to, or picked up by the vehicle owners. Thus, it is normally illegal for private citizens to make and affix their own plates, because such unauthorized private manufacturing is equivalent to [forging](/wiki/Forgery "Forgery") an official document. Alternatively, the government will merely assign plate numbers, and it is the vehicle owner's responsibility to find an approved private supplier to make a plate with that number.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Additionally, citizens can create custom plates, following specific guidelines and naming conventions approved by the relevant national authorities (such as the DVLA in the UK).[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In some jurisdictions, plates will be permanently assigned to that particular vehicle for its lifetime. If the vehicle is either destroyed or exported to a different jurisdiction, the plate number is retired or reissued; exported vehicles must be re-registered in the jurisdiction of import. China requires the re-registration of any vehicle that crosses its borders from another country, such as for overland tourist visits, regardless of the length of time it is due to remain there; this has to be arranged with prior approval.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Other jurisdictions follow a "plate-to-owner" policy, meaning that when a vehicle is sold the seller removes the current plate(s) from the vehicle. Buyers must either obtain new plates or attach plates they already hold, as well as register their vehicles under the buyer's name and plate number. A person who sells a car and then purchases a new one can apply to have the old plates put onto the new car. One who sells a car and does not buy a new one may, depending on the local laws involved, have to turn the old plates in or destroy them, or may be permitted to keep them. Some jurisdictions permit the registration of the vehicle with "personal" ("vanity" or "cherished mark") plates.

In some jurisdictions, plates require periodic replacement, often associated with a design change of the plate itself. Vehicle owners may or may not have the option to keep their original plate number, and may have to pay a fee to exercise this option. Alternately, or additionally, vehicle owners have to replace a small [decal](/wiki/Decal "Decal") on the plate or use a decal on the windshield to indicate the expiration date of the [vehicle registration](/wiki/Vehicle_registration "Vehicle registration"), periodic safety and/or emissions inspections or vehicle taxation. Other jurisdictions have replaced the decal requirement through the use of computerization: a central database maintains records of which plate numbers are associated with expired registrations, communicating with [automated number plate readers](/wiki/Automated_number_plate_recognition "Automated number plate recognition") to enable law-enforcement to identify expired registrations in the field.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Thai_registration_plate_with_a_Malaysian_plate_at_the_bottom.jpg/250px-Thai_registration_plate_with_a_Malaysian_plate_at_the_bottom.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thai_registration_plate_with_a_Malaysian_plate_at_the_bottom.jpg)

A Thai registration plate with a Malaysian numberplate sticker directly below the car to allow for cross-border travel from Thailand to Malaysia

Plates are usually fixed directly to a vehicle or to a plate frame that is fixed to the vehicle. Sometimes, the plate frames contain advertisements inserted by the vehicle service centre or the dealership from which the vehicle was purchased. Vehicle owners can also purchase customized frames to replace the original frames. In some jurisdictions registration plate frames are illegal or have design restrictions. For example, many states, like Texas, allow plate frames but prohibit plate frames from covering the name of the state, province, district, Native American tribe or country that issued the license plate (when that information appears on the plate). Plates are designed to conform to standards with regard to being read by eye in day or at night, or by electronic equipment. Some drivers purchase clear, smoke-colored or tinted covers that go over the registration plate to prevent electronic equipment from scanning the registration plate. Legality of these covers varies. Some cameras incorporate filter systems that make such avoidance attempts unworkable, usually with infra-red filters.

Vehicles pulling [trailers](/wiki/Trailer_(vehicle) "Trailer (vehicle)"), such as [caravans](/wiki/Caravan_(towed_trailer) "Caravan (towed trailer)") and [semi-trailer trucks](/wiki/Semi-trailer_truck "Semi-trailer truck"), are typically required to display a third registration plate on the rear of the trailer.

## Plate design considerations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Plate design considerations")]

An engineering study by the [University of Illinois](/wiki/University_of_Illinois "University of Illinois") published in 1960 recommended that the state of Illinois adopt a numbering system and plate design "composed of combinations of characters which can be perceived quickly and accurately, are legible at a distance of approximately 125 feet (38 m) under daylight conditions, and are readily adapted to filing and administrative procedures". It also recommended that a standard plate size of 6 inches (15 cm) by 14 inches (36 cm) be adopted through the United States to replace the earlier 6 inches (15 cm) by 12 inches (30 cm) size to allow longer registration numbers to be displayed without excessively tight spacing or excessively thin or narrow characters.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In order to combat registration plate fraud, from the 1920s several jurisdictions developed their own [anti-fraud](/wiki/Anti-fraud "Anti-fraud") typefaces so that characters cannot be painted or modified to resemble other characters. Since the 1990s, many jurisdictions have adopted the [FE-Schrift](/wiki/FE-Schrift "FE-Schrift") typeface.

English uses twenty-six letters (languages such as [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"), [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language") and [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") have more letters), so assuming that the letters vs. digits must appear in particular locations (common on plates in most jurisdictions, for instance four decimal digits and two letters where the letters must come first, allowing AB1234 but excluding A12B34), allowing for repeating letters and digits, the combinations for each of these will be:

Possible combinations

| Combinations possible with particular arrangements | All possible combinations | Digits | Letters | Sample |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2,600,000 | 15,600,000 | 5 | 1 | 123 45A |
| 26,000,000 | 182,000,000 | 6 | 1 | 123 A 456 |
| 6,760,000 | 101,400,000 | 4 | 2 | AB 12 34 |
| 67,600,000 | 1,419,600,000 | 5 | 2 | AB 123 45 |
| 17,576,000 | 351,520,000 | 3 | 3 | ABC 123 |
| 175,760,000 | 6,151,600,000 | 4 | 3 | 12 ABC 34 |
| 456,976,000 | 15,994,160,000 | 3 | 4 | AB 123 CD |

If letters and digits can appear in any order, it is problematic to allow both the letter O and the digit 0 to be used. Even if the license plate uses distinguishable characters for the two, someone transcribing the plate may not know which symbol has which meaning, and the owner of plate EM6F9VO may get in trouble for something the owner of plate EM6F9V0 did. Other letter–digit pairs, like I and 1, may be similarly problematic to a lesser degree. Allowing for repeating letters and digits, the combinations for each of these will be:

Possible combinations

| Combinations possible with *digit 0* excluded | Combinations possible with *letter O* excluded | Digits | Letters |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 9,211,644 | 15,000,000 | 5 | 1 |
| 96,722,262 | 175,000,000 | 6 | 1 |
| 66,528,540 | 93,750,000 | 4 | 2 |
| 838,259,604 | 1,312,500,000 | 5 | 2 |
| 256,258,080 | 312,500,000 | 3 | 3 |
| 4,036,064,760 | 5,468,750,000 | 4 | 3 |
| 11,659,742,640 | 13,671,875,000 | 3 | 4 |

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

[France](/wiki/France "France") was the first country to introduce the registration plate with the enactment of the Paris Police Ordinance on 14 August 1893,[[8]](#cite_note-Robertson1974-8) followed by [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") in 1896.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") was the first country to introduce a national registration plate, called a "driving permit", in 1898. Initially these plates were just sequentially numbered, starting at 1, but this was changed in 1906.

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), where each state issues plates, [New York State](/wiki/New_York_State "New York State") has required plates since 1903 (black numerals on a white background) after first requiring in 1901 only that the owner's initials be clearly visible on the back of the vehicle.[[10]](#cite_note-10) At first, plates were not government-issued in most [jurisdictions](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") and [motorists](/wiki/Driving "Driving") were obliged to make their own. In 1903, [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") was the first state to issue plates. In 1928, [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho") was the first state to put a logo on the plate (the "Idaho Potato").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), the first law to define rules on non-animal vehicle traction was *Real orden de 1897 de circulación de vehículos cuyo motor no sea la fuerza animal*[[12]](#cite_note-12) and the registration of vehicles was defined as a provincial task in the *Reglamento de 1900 para el servicio de coches automóviles por las carreteras*.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The first Spanish registration plate, PM–1[[15]](#cite_note-15), was issued for a [Clément-Talbot](/wiki/Cl%C3%A9ment-Talbot "Clément-Talbot") on 31 October 1900 in [Palma de Mallorca](/wiki/Palma_de_Mallorca "Palma de Mallorca").[[16]](#cite_note-Espana-16) 256 vehicles were registered between 1901 and 1905.[[16]](#cite_note-Espana-16)

### Materials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Materials")]

The earliest plates were made of [enamel](/wiki/Vitreous_enamel "Vitreous enamel") on metal or ceramic with no backing, which made them fragile and impractical.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Few of these early plates survived.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Later experimental materials include [cardboard](/wiki/Paperboard "Paperboard"), [leather](/wiki/Leather "Leather"), [plastic](/wiki/Plastic "Plastic"), and, during wartime shortages, [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper") and pressed [soybeans](/wiki/Soybean "Soybean").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

  In 1944, [Wyoming license plates](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Wyoming "Vehicle registration plates of Wyoming") were made of soybean-based fiberboard due to metal conservation for World War II.

The first automobile license plate in Minnesota, 1903.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/First_Minnesotan_license_plate.jpg/250px-First_Minnesotan_license_plate.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Minnesotan_license_plate.jpg "Black leather automobile license tag with a brass number on the center front. The first automobile license plate in Minnesota, 1903.")

  Black leather automobile license tag with a brass number on the center front. The first automobile license plate in Minnesota, 1903.

As of the 21st century, most plates are made out of [aluminium](/wiki/Aluminium "Aluminium").[[17]](#cite_note-Bossier_Page_377-17) Metal plates are manufactured through one of two processes: embossing or riveting.[[17]](#cite_note-Bossier_Page_377-17) For embossing, a plate is placed between dies on each side corresponding to the desired characters and compressed by a press.[[18]](#cite_note-Bossier_Page_378-18) For riveting, holes are drilled through the plate and then individual letters are riveted one-by-one to the plate.[[19]](#cite_note-Bossier_Page_379-19)

New technology has allowed for the development of digital license plates. In 2018, Michigan approved Public Act 656, making electronic license plates legal.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/License_plate_sizes.svg/500px-License_plate_sizes.svg.png)](/wiki/File:License_plate_sizes.svg)

License plate size comparison

Early 20th century plates varied in size and shape from one jurisdiction to the next, such that if someone moved or the car was resold in a new area, new holes would need to be drilled into the automobile (often on the bumper) to support the new plate, or an adapter plate be made. [Standardization](/wiki/Standardization "Standardization") of plates came in 1957, when automobile manufacturers came to agreement with governments and international standards organizations. While peculiar local variants exist, there are four basic standards worldwide:

* 520 by 110 mm (20.5 by 4.3 inches) or 520 by 120 mm (20.5 by 4.7 inches) – in the bulk of [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") countries and many of their former overseas territories, as well as [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea").
* 400 by 130 mm (15.7 by 5.1 inches) – in the [Mercosur](/wiki/Mercosur "Mercosur") member countries: [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay"), and [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay"), originally based on the 1990 Brazilian standard.
* 300 by 150 mm (11.8 by 5.9 inches) – in the majority of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") and [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), and parts of [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"); occasionally in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"); and many [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") countries, also allowed for imported vehicles in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").
* 372 by 135 mm (14.6 by 5.3 inches) – in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and some other [Pacific Rim](/wiki/Pacific_Rim "Pacific Rim") countries, about halfway between the dimensions of the other two standards, longer than Western Hemisphere plates but taller than European ones.

Additional sizes include:

* 275–240 by 200–220 mm (10.8–9.4 by 7.9–8.7 inches) – two line pattern, optional in several European countries for 4×4 and imported vehicles;
* 290 by 170 mm (11.4 by 6.7 inches) – in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") for imported Japanese and American vehicles.
* 300 by 80 mm (11.8 by 3.1 inches) – in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") and [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein").
* 310 by 155 mm (12.2 by 6.1 inches) – in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia").
* 330 by 140 mm (13.0 by 5.5 inches) – in [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra").
* In [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"):[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
  + from 330 by 165 mm (13.0 by 6.5 inches) to 520 by 110 mm (20.5 by 4.3 inches) – for car,
  + 190 by 140 mm (7.5 by 5.5 inches) – for bike.
* 330 by 165 mm (13.0 by 6.5 inches) – in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* In [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"):
  + 340 by 110 mm (13.4 by 4.3 inches) – for the rear,
  + 260 by 90 mm (10.2 by 3.5 inches) – for the front.
* 340 by 150 mm (13.4 by 5.9 inches) – in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").
* 360 by 110 mm (14.2 by 4.3 inches) – in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") (front plates), and [Oman](/wiki/Oman "Oman").
* 360 by 125 mm (14.2 by 4.9 inches) – in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand").
* 360 by 130 mm (14.2 by 5.1 inches) – in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile").
* 380 by 160 mm (15.0 by 6.3 inches) – in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan").
* 390 by 120 mm (15.4 by 4.7 inches) – in [San Marino](/wiki/San_Marino "San Marino").
* 390 by 140 mm (15.4 by 5.5 inches) – in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines")
* 404 by 154 mm (15.9 by 6.1 inches) – in [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador").
* 430 by 110 mm (16.9 by 4.3 inches) – in [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan").
* In [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")
  + 275 by 110 mm (10.8 by 4.3 inches) – for two-or-three-wheeled vehicles, in the front and rear.
  + 460 by 135 mm (18.1 by 5.3 inches) – for four-or-more-wheeled vehicles, in the front and rear.
* 440 by 120 mm (17.3 by 4.7 inches) – in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland").
* 440 by 140 mm (17.3 by 5.5 inches) – in [China](/wiki/China "China"), but 480 by 140 mm (18.9 by 5.5 inches) for [New Energy vehicles](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_China#New_Energy_vehicles_license_plates "Vehicle registration plates of China").
* 450 by 100 mm (17.7 by 3.9 inches) – in [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria")[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] and [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia "Tunisia") (front plates).

Previous sizes included:

* 190 by 140 mm (7.5 by 5.5 inches) – in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") before 2021 for all vehicles.
* 320 by 90 mm (12.6 by 3.5 inches) – in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") before 2024.
* 320 by 150 mm (12.6 by 5.9 inches) – in [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay "Paraguay") and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan")[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*].
* 330 by 155 mm (13.0 by 6.1 inches) – in [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea")[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*].
* 340 by 110 mm (13.4 by 4.3 inches) – in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") before 2013.
* 340 by 120 mm (13.4 by 4.7 inches) – in [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") before 2003.
* 350 by 155 mm (13.8 by 6.1 inches) – in [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") before 2016.
* 390 by 120 mm (15.4 by 4.7 inches) – in [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") before 2001.
* 420 by 120 mm (16.5 by 4.7 inches) – in [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") before 2011, based on [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia")'s standard.

### Botswana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Botswana")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Botswana-rear-plate.jpg/250px-Botswana-rear-plate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Botswana-rear-plate.jpg)

[Botswana plate](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Botswana "Vehicle registration plates of Botswana")

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Botswana](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Botswana "Vehicle registration plates of Botswana")

Normal vehicles have number plates starting with the letter *B*, followed by three digits, followed by three letters. The digits and letters are assigned by a registrar. The three letters never include the letter *Q*, to avoid confusion with *O*. [Botswana](/wiki/Botswana "Botswana") number plates have a reflective white front and yellow rear background, and black lettering.

Government vehicles all have the prefix "BX", these number plates have a white reflective background with red lettering at the front and white on red at the rear. After 'BX' are the last two numerals of the date of issue and then up to four digits issued serially.

Botswana Defence Force vehicles have the prefix "BDF" in white on an 'army' green background.

Diplomatic vehicles' number plates start with two numerals which indicate the embassy to which they are attached, then two letters CD (Corps Diplomatique), CC (Consular Corps status) or CT (Foreign Technical and Advisory personnel) and another three digits which are serial. The official car of the Head of Mission uses the letters CMD rather than CD and the private vehicle uses CDA.
This series is allocated by the Minister of Foreign Affairs.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Botswana is the former British Protectorate of [Bechuanaland](/wiki/Bechuanaland "Bechuanaland"), and number plates then used a 'BP' prefix (then BPA, BPB, etc.) followed by up to three digits, in white on black background, the plates being made in the characteristic style of South Africa at that time.

### Burkina Faso

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Burkina Faso")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Burkina Faso](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Burkina_Faso "Vehicle registration plates of Burkina Faso")

[![GJ 34](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Burkina_Faso_passenger_vehicle_plate.jpg/250px-Burkina_Faso_passenger_vehicle_plate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burkina_Faso_passenger_vehicle_plate.jpg)

Burkina Faso passenger plate

Vehicles are fitted with registration plates in the front and rear of the vehicle. Motorcycles (50cc or more) must be licensed and only bear rear plates. Registration is performed at the local police or Gendarmerie station. The first digits of the plate indicate the province in which the vehicle is registered. Only plates which meet government standards and are sold by licensed dealers may be fitted.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Private passenger car registration plates have a white background with black letters and digits. Plates exist in a long pattern and a rectangular pattern, similar in size and appearance to French plates. The plate is adorned with a small flag of Burkina Faso in the shape of the country, inscribed in a black circle. The letters "BF" appear below the circle, also in black. This circle and BF design is to the right of the long plate and to the upper right of the rectangular plate. Motorcycle plates are similar to rectangular automobile plates, but smaller.

Commercial registration plates are similar in appearance to private plates, but the background is blue, and the writing and circle are white.

Security forces plates are black with white letters. They are adorned with the emblem of the relevant security service.

Cameroonian license plate

The current scheme of regular license plates was introduced in 1985. It has AB1234V formats or AB123VH where AB is the code region, 1234 is the number, and SH is the series. The regular plate has an orange background with black marks. Since 2005, to reflect the German [FE font](/wiki/FE-Schrift "FE-Schrift") used, on the left side of the plate are the emblems of the Economic Community of Central African States code and the CMR emblem.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/KENYA_passenger_license_plate_1950-1989_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg/250px-KENYA_passenger_license_plate_1950-1989_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg)](/wiki/File:KENYA_passenger_license_plate_1950-1989_Flickr_-_woody1778a.jpg)

An old Kenyan number plate from the 1970s

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Kenya](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Kenya "Vehicle registration plates of Kenya")

The current series of **vehicle registration plates** in **[Kenya](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya")** are on a white plate with black lettering and look quite similar to UK suffix style registrations. The format is LLL 000L, where 'L' denotes a letter and '0' denotes a digit. The older series of number plates were black with white or silver lettering. The rear plates in the older series of number plates were yellow and black lettering. According to Kenya National Bureau of Statistics there are over 1,626,380 vehicles on Kenyan roads as at 2011.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Morocco")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Morocco_license_plate_2451%D8%A3_Agadir_Inezgane_-_Ait_Melloul.jpg/250px-Morocco_license_plate_2451%D8%A3_Agadir_Inezgane_-_Ait_Melloul.jpg)](/wiki/File:Morocco_license_plate_2451%D8%A3_Agadir_Inezgane_-_Ait_Melloul.jpg)

[Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), [Agadir](/wiki/Agadir "Agadir") registration plate

New-style (post-1983, black lettering on white) [Moroccan](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco") vehicle registrations have one or two digits to the right of the plate to indicate the town of registration.
Each number is separated by a vertical line. To the left of the plate are a series of up to 5 digits issued consecutively. These are separated from the town of registration digits by a hyphen.

Earlier plates (1972–1983, black lettering on white. Pre-1972, white lettering on black) differed in that they could have either one or two numbers to indicate the town of registration. The group of digits was separated from the rest of the plate by a vertical line.

The current plates use numerals without script. Earlier plates used numerals and included [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script").

### Argentina

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Argentina")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Argentina](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Argentina "Vehicle registration plates of Argentina")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_argentina_2016_%28Mercosur%29-B.png/250px-Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_argentina_2016_%28Mercosur%29-B.png)](/wiki/File:Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_argentina_2016_(Mercosur)-B.png)

[Argentinian](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") registration plate

The history of registration plates in Argentina can be broken down in two major phases: the decentralized phase (until 1972) and the centralized one (since 1972). During the decentralized phase, registration plates were assigned by each municipality or by the provinces, while during the second phase, the national state took charge of standardizing and centralizing the design and style.

Argentina has used the **ABC 123** format since 1995. However, from 2016, new registration plates with the logo of Mercosur and the **AB 123 CD** format were implemented. Both formats coexist temporarily.[[25]](#cite_note-auto-25)

### Bolivia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Bolivia")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Bolivia](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Bolivia "Vehicle registration plates of Bolivia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_Bolivia_2006_1852PHD_La_Paz.jpg/250px-Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_Bolivia_2006_1852PHD_La_Paz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_Bolivia_2006_1852PHD_La_Paz.jpg)

Bolivian registration plate issued in [La Paz](/wiki/La_Paz "La Paz"), as indicated by the "L" sticker in the top right corner

[Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia")'s current registration plate system consists of four numbers followed by three letters. At the top of the plate, "BOLIVIA" is spelled out. At the top left corner, the Bolivian flag may be present, and at the top right corner, a letter denoting the department in which the car is registered, according to the [ISO 3166-2:BO](/wiki/ISO_3166-2:BO "ISO 3166-2:BO") code, is displayed on either a metal tab on older plates or a sticker on newer plates. The current registration plate design consists of a white background with a blue borderline and blue letters and numbers.

Serial digits progress sequentially from right to left, with the 000 AAA format followed by the **1**000AAA format and currently the **4**000AAA format.

Older plate serials consisted of three numbers followed by three letters (A to Z, except O and Q). They had a white background with black letters and numbers.

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Brazil")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Brazil](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Brazil "Vehicle registration plates of Brazil")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Brazilian_vehicle_license_plate_%282018-%29.jpg/250px-Brazilian_vehicle_license_plate_%282018-%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brazilian_vehicle_license_plate_(2018-).jpg)

Plate for a private vehicle issued in 2018 from [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), Brazil

[Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") adopted its former system in 1990, which uses the form ABC•1234, with a dot between the letters and the numbers. A combination given to a particular vehicle follows it from first issue to the scrapyard: it cannot be transferred to another vehicle. Above the combination there was a metallic band with the state abbreviation (SP = [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)"), RJ = [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Rio de Janeiro (state)"), PR = [Paraná](/wiki/Paran%C3%A1_(state) "Paraná (state)"), AM = [Amazonas](/wiki/Amazonas_(Brazilian_state) "Amazonas (Brazilian state)"), etc.) and the name of the municipality in which the vehicle title owner resides. During the first registration of a new vehicle, the registering state issues a registration plate to the vehicle rather than owner, and the serial stays with the vehicle for its life.

Brazil, as a member of [Mercosur](/wiki/Mercosur "Mercosur"), from September 2018 on, began a new registration system where plates have a blue band at the top with the logo of Mercosur on the left, the country's name centered and [country flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Brazil "Flag of Brazil") on the right. On the bottom left, there is the international vehicle registration code for Brazil: **BR**. The plates are always white: the letter coloring indicates the category (e.g., black on white: private; blue on white: official, police, fire departments, etc.; red on white: taxis, buses, paid freight, etc.). A new format based on the previous one, ABC1D23, was implemented. All used cars, when transferred to another owner, must change to the new format keeping their registration, where only its second number (the fifth position of the alphanumeric combination) shall change to a letter, following the pattern: 0=A, 1=B, 2=C, 3=D, ... 9=J. As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit), both formats coexist for the time being.[[25]](#cite_note-auto-25)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Canada")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Canada](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Canada "Vehicle registration plates of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/2016_Ontario_License_Plate_CBPC-344.jpg/250px-2016_Ontario_License_Plate_CBPC-344.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_Ontario_License_Plate_CBPC-344.jpg)

An Ontario licence plate, demonstrating the standardised North American size

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Canada_Federal_license_plate_78579.jpg/250px-Canada_Federal_license_plate_78579.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canada_Federal_license_plate_78579.jpg)

A Canadian plate used by the federal government

In Canada, license plates are issued on the provincial or territorial level. Federally issued plates are only used by the [Department of National Defence](/wiki/Department_of_National_Defence_(Canada) "Department of National Defence (Canada)"). All provinces issue plates in which the letters and numbers are embossed so that they are slightly raised above its surface. The territory of [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut") introduced the first flat registration plate in Canada in July 2012.

In the Canadian provinces and territories of [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta"), [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick"), [Newfoundland and Labrador](/wiki/Newfoundland_and_Labrador "Newfoundland and Labrador"), the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"), [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut"), [Prince Edward Island](/wiki/Prince_Edward_Island "Prince Edward Island"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), [Saskatchewan](/wiki/Saskatchewan "Saskatchewan"), and the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), registration plates are currently only required on the rear of the vehicle. The remaining provinces – [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), [Manitoba](/wiki/Manitoba "Manitoba"), and [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") – require the registration plates to be mounted on both the front and rear of the vehicle, with the exception of some special registration classes in BC such as dealer licenses and "collector floater" plates where only one plate is issued. When a person moves from one province to another, they are normally required to obtain new registration plates issued by the new place of residence, with some agreements between individual provinces allowing temporary workers, and post secondary students to keep their home plates.

In 1956, all North American passenger vehicle registration plates (except for French-controlled [St. Pierre and Miquelon](/wiki/St._Pierre_and_Miquelon "St. Pierre and Miquelon")), were standardized at a size of 6 in × 12 in (152 mm × 305 mm), although a smaller size is used for certain vehicle classes, such as [motorcycles](/wiki/Motorcycle "Motorcycle"), and for the state of Delaware's historic alternate black and white plates, which are 5.25 in × 9.5 in (133 mm × 241 mm). The plates of the Northwest Territories are shaped like a [polar bear](/wiki/Polar_bear "Polar bear") but bolt to the standard holes. Nunavut inherited this design on its creation but switched to rectangular plates in 2012.[[26]](#cite_note-26) [Canadian Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Forces "Canadian Forces") vehicles that travel on regular roads display registration plates. These vehicles have registration plates issued by the [Department of National Defence](/wiki/Department_of_National_Defence_(Canada) "Department of National Defence (Canada)"). Domestic plates were issued by the DND after 1968.

Most Canadian provinces offer personalized or vanity license plates, where one can display their own unique combination of characters.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Prison inmates in Ontario make registration plates.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories") issues licence plates in a [unique polar bear–shaped design](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_the_Northwest_Territories "Vehicle registration plates of the Northwest Territories").

### Ecuador

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Ecuador")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Ecuador](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Ecuador "Vehicle registration plates of Ecuador")

Vehicle number plates in Ecuador have only one design, with the word "Ecuador" appearing in uppercase letters centred at the top of the plate and a unique letter-number combination which consists of three letters (except for motorbikes, diplomatic vehicles and vehicles owned by international organisations) followed by three or more numerical digits. Formats in use are ABC-123 (old format) and ABC-1234 (new format).

The first letter in the letter-number combination indicates the province of issue. The second letter might be sequential or a "key letter" identifying the type of registration plate; the following letters and numbers are assigned in sequential order to the date the vehicle has been registered.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Mexico")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Mexico](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Mexico "Vehicle registration plates of Mexico")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_M%C3%A9xico_2004_Baja_California_AHL-71-17_europlaca_legaloid.jpg/250px-Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_M%C3%A9xico_2004_Baja_California_AHL-71-17_europlaca_legaloid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_M%C3%A9xico_2004_Baja_California_AHL-71-17_europlaca_legaloid.jpg)

[Baja Californian](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") Euro-style registration plate (unofficial)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_M%C3%A9xico_2002_Sonora_VUK-17-75.jpg/250px-Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_M%C3%A9xico_2002_Sonora_VUK-17-75.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matr%C3%ADcula_automovil%C3%ADstica_M%C3%A9xico_2002_Sonora_VUK-17-75.jpg)

[Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") – [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora") registration plate

Each [Mexican](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") state issues registration plates of a different design. Most states change designs more or less every third year, with each state on its own plate replacement cycle. Every year Mexicans pay the *tenencia* or *revalidación de placas* ("car plates renewal tax"). A set of Mexican plates includes one pair of plates, a windshield sticker, and in a few states a plate sticker. In 2001 the size of the plate number was reduced to accommodate the addition of the state number, a legend indicating the position of the plate on the vehicle (*delantera* (front) or *trasera* (rear)), and additional graphics. European-sized plates do exist in Mexico, but are not official or legal.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[*[self-published source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] These generally contain the same design as the standard-size plate in use at the time, and bear the standard letter and number sequence.

Mexican plates come in several different classifications: private, private border, public, public border, federal public service, fiscal and customs inspection, Mexico Army, and diplomatic. The border plates were introduced in 1972 and are available in the Mexico–USA border zone. This zone is formed by the states of [Baja California](/wiki/Baja_California "Baja California") and [Baja California Sur](/wiki/Baja_California_Sur "Baja California Sur"), as well as parts of [Sonora](/wiki/Sonora "Sonora"), [Chihuahua](/wiki/Chihuahua_(state) "Chihuahua (state)"), [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila"), and [Tamaulipas](/wiki/Tamaulipas "Tamaulipas"). While the state of [Nuevo León](/wiki/Nuevo_Le%C3%B3n "Nuevo León") shares a 15 km (9 mi) border with the U.S., it does not have any cities within the border zone.

### Panama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Panama")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Panama](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Panama "Vehicle registration plates of Panama")

In Panama, the design of registration plates changes every year and the plates are the standard North American size and shape. They used to be made by prison inmates but are now produced on demand and on site using a press. Plates used to have 6 numbers, but since 2013 new plates for new vehicles have 2 letters and 4 numbers. The 2 letters go before the numbers. They have a sticker on one of the corners indicating in which month of the year the plate was issued. On the bottom center of the plate is the year when the plate was issued. They also have holograms on the right edge of the plate. On the top center the plate has the word PANAMA. Government vehicles always begin with GO. Taxis begin with T and are always yellow. School buses say COLEGIAL instead of the year the plate was issued. Motorcycles begin with M. Metro buses begin with MB. Plates for motorcycles are about half the standard size. Plates of vehicles belonging to the national assembly are always white and have the logo of the national assembly on the left and two numbers on the right and are always white. Vehicles of government executives have no registration plate. Vehicles from Central America can be used freely in Panama without having to change plates but only if the vehicle will not be staying indefinitely in Panama. Vehicles must be checked every year when changing the plate. The plate must be changed every year and once the vehicle is checked a sticker must be attached to the back of the vehicle's windshield. The color of the sticker changes every year.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: United States")]

Main article: [Vehicle license plates of the United States](/wiki/Vehicle_license_plates_of_the_United_States "Vehicle license plates of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/California_license_plate%2C_August_2012.png/250px-California_license_plate%2C_August_2012.png)](/wiki/File:California_license_plate,_August_2012.png)

Standard [California](/wiki/California "California") plate as of 2011

The first registration plates in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") appeared in 1903 in the [Commonwealth](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"). Soon after, other states followed suit, with virtually every state having adopted a form of registration plates by 1918.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The first registration plates in the United States were made out of [leather](/wiki/Leather "Leather"), [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber"), [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), and [porcelain](/wiki/Porcelain "Porcelain"), painted on the front in usually two different colors – one for the background and one for the lettering. This scheme held true for most states until about 1920. The front of the plate would usually contain the registration number in large digits, and in smaller lettering on one side of the plate, the two- or four-digit year number, and an abbreviated state name. Each year, citizens were usually required to obtain a new registration plate from the state government, which would have a different color scheme than the previous year, making it easier for police to identify whether citizens were current with their vehicle registration.

Even before 1920, some states had adopted the technique of [embossing](/wiki/Embossing_(manufacturing) "Embossing (manufacturing)") the metal plates with raised lettering and numbering, without porcelain, and applying [paint](/wiki/Paint "Paint") all over the plate, directly onto the metal. [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") introduced some registration plates during this period with three different years embossed into the plate, so that the plates were valid for three consecutive years (e.g., 1918, 1919, and 1920).

In the United States, registration plates are issued by each state.[[33]](#cite_note-Bossier_Page_375-33) The [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") issues plates only for its own [vehicle fleet](/wiki/Vehicle_fleet "Vehicle fleet") and for vehicles owned by [foreign diplomats](/wiki/Diplomat "Diplomat"). In the United States, many [Native American tribal governments](/wiki/Tribal_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Tribal sovereignty in the United States") issue [plates for their members](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Indian_tribes "Vehicle registration plates of Indian tribes"), while some states provide special issues for tribal members. Within each jurisdiction, there may also be special plates for groups such as firefighters or military veterans, and for state or municipality-owned vehicles.

The appearance of plates is frequently chosen to contain symbols or slogans associated with the issuing [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction").[[34]](#cite_note-Bossier_Page_376-34) Some of these are intended to promote the region. A few make political statements; for example, most plates issued in [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia") include the phrase "[Taxation Without Representation](/wiki/Taxation_Without_Representation "Taxation Without Representation")" to highlight D.C.'s lack of a voting representative in the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"). More recently, some states have also started to put a [web address](/wiki/Web_address "Web address") pertaining to the state (such as [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), which posts the address of its [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") site). In some states ([Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"), [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi"), [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), and some versions in [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida")), the issuing [county](/wiki/County "County") is listed at the bottom, while [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas") does so with a letter-coded registration sticker; [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") did so until 2003. [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") identifies counties with a two-digit code in the lower right corner of its plates. [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama"), [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), most [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"), and some [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") standard issue plates designate the county by unique codes, usually numeric (Idaho uses a one-letter or one-number/one-letter code; Oklahoma uses a one-letter code), either in the plate number or registration sticker. Some states, such as [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire"), [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), and [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), formerly used county-coded or county-labeled plates before switching to standard-progression plates.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/2022_Tennessee_License_Plate_Base_-_Standard_Automobile.png/250px-2022_Tennessee_License_Plate_Base_-_Standard_Automobile.png)](/wiki/File:2022_Tennessee_License_Plate_Base_-_Standard_Automobile.png)

Standard [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee") plate, with the county name, seen in use in February 2022

Most states use plates onto which the letters and numbers are embossed so that they are slightly raised above its surface. Characters on [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") plates are [engraved](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") onto a large, slightly raised portion of the plate. Seventeen states – Alabama, [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona "Arizona"), [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), Georgia, Idaho, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas,[[35]](#cite_note-35) Minnesota, Montana, Nebraska, [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), Oklahoma, [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina"), South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Wyoming – and both the District of Columbia and Puerto Rico, have moved to entirely digitally printed "flat" registration plates. Several other states, such as [Colorado](/wiki/Colorado "Colorado"), Mississippi, [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), New York, Ohio, [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), and [West Virginia](/wiki/West_Virginia "West Virginia") produce a flat registration plate only for certain plates, such as [personalized license plates](/wiki/Personalized_license_plates "Personalized license plates") and special interest plates. [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") reverted from using flat registration plates to using embossed plates, after using flat plates as a standard issue for a few years. Additionally, [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") used flat plates for their endangered resources specialty plates from 2010 to 2016.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Pennsylvania_2017_license_plate.jpg/250px-Pennsylvania_2017_license_plate.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pennsylvania_2017_license_plate.jpg)

Standard [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") plate using sample lettering

The numbering system of registration plates also varies among the jurisdictions. Some states issue a motorist a serial that stays with that person as long as they live in that state, while other states periodically issue new serials and completely rotate out any old ones. Some states issue registration plates to vehicles rather than owners, and the serial stays with the vehicle for its life. Several states do not regularly use certain letters – most commonly the letters [I](/wiki/I "I"), [O](/wiki/O "O"), and/or [Q](/wiki/Q "Q") – in their plates, except on [vanity plates](/wiki/Vanity_plates "Vanity plates"), so as not to confuse observers with the numbers one and zero.

When a person moves from one state to another, they are normally required to obtain new registration plates issued by the new place of residence.[[33]](#cite_note-Bossier_Page_375-33) Some U.S. states will even require a person to obtain new plates if they accept employment in that state, unless they can show that they return to another state to live on a regular basis. The most prominent exceptions to this policy are active duty military service members, who legally do not change residence when they move to a new posting. Federal law specifically allows them to choose to either retain the state vehicle registration of their original residence or change registration to their state of assignment.

In the United States, 22 states – Alabama, Alaska, Arizona, [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"), Delaware, Florida, Georgia, Indiana, Kansas, Kentucky, [Louisiana](/wiki/Louisiana "Louisiana"), [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), Mississippi, [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), [North Carolina](/wiki/North_Carolina "North Carolina"), Ohio, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Tennessee, Utah, and West Virginia – do not require an official front registration plate. In Nevada, front plates are optional if the vehicle was not designed for a front plate and the manufacturer did not provide an add-on bracket or other means of displaying the front plate.[[38]](#cite_note-38) In [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), certain old rear-only plates are grandfathered, but newly issued registrations require both front and rear plates. Vehicles owned by the [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service"), unlike other federally owned civilian vehicles, do not bear registration plates, but rather a postal service number such as on the [Grumman LLV](/wiki/Grumman_LLV "Grumman LLV").

In 1956, all North American passenger vehicle registration plates, except for French-controlled [St. Pierre and Miquelon](/wiki/St._Pierre_and_Miquelon "St. Pierre and Miquelon"), were standardized at a size of 6 in × 12 in (152.40 mm × 304.80 mm), although a smaller size is used for certain vehicle classes, such as [motorcycles](/wiki/Motorcycle "Motorcycle"), and for the state of Delaware's historic alternate black and white plates, which are 5.25 in × 9.5 in (133.35 mm × 241.30 mm). However, in 2012 Puerto Rico began issuing optional [European-style plates](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Europe#Format "Vehicle registration plates of Europe") that incorporate the design language of the standard-issue plates in a longer and narrower size typically seen in Europe.

Tactical vehicles of the United States military do not bear registration plates, even if they travel regularly on public streets and highways.

In many U.S. states, registration plates are made by prison inmates.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Because of this, colloquial terms include "license plate factories" for prison and "making license plates" for serving a prison sentence.

### Bangladesh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Bangladesh")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Bangladesh](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Bangladesh "Vehicle registration plates of Bangladesh")

[Bangladeshi](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") registration plates use [Bengali alphabets](/wiki/Bengali_alphabet "Bengali alphabet") and [Bengali numerals](/wiki/Bengali_numerals "Bengali numerals").
In [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), the [Road Transport Authority (BRTA)](/wiki/Bangladesh_Road_Transport_Authority "Bangladesh Road Transport Authority") issues vehicle registration plates for motor vehicles. The vehicle registration plates in Bangladesh use [Bengali alphabets](/wiki/Bengali_alphabet "Bengali alphabet") and [Bengali numerals](/wiki/Bengali_numerals "Bengali numerals"). The current version of Vehicle registration plates started in 1973. The International vehicle registration code for Bangladesh is **BD**.

The general format of vehicle registration plates in Bangladesh is "City – Vehicle Class alphabet and No – Vehicle No". For example, : "DHAKA-D-11-9999". The "DHAKA" field represents the city name in [Bengali alphabets](/wiki/Bengali_alphabet "Bengali alphabet"), the "D" field represents the vehicle class in [Bengali alphabets](/wiki/Bengali_alphabet "Bengali alphabet"), the "11" field represents the vehicle registration serial in [Bengali numerals](/wiki/Bengali_numerals "Bengali numerals") (newer registrations have a higher serial number) and finally, the "9999" field represents the vehicle number of the vehicle in [Bengali numerals](/wiki/Bengali_numerals "Bengali numerals").

The plates are installed in both the front and rear of the vehicle, with the rear plate permanently attached to the vehicle. The plate is only removed when the vehicle has reached the end of service and has been sold for scrap. New vehicles are not delivered to the purchaser until the plates have been attached at the dealership.

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: China")]

#### Mainland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Mainland")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of China](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_China "Vehicle registration plates of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/%E6%B9%98_license_plate_of_Hunan%2C_People%27s_Republic_of_China.jpg/250px-%E6%B9%98_license_plate_of_Hunan%2C_People%27s_Republic_of_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%B9%98_license_plate_of_Hunan,_People%27s_Republic_of_China.jpg)

Blue [PRC](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_China "Vehicle registration plates of China") registration plate of the 1992 standard

The [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") issues vehicles registration plates at its Vehicle Management Offices, under the administration of the Ministry of Public Security.

The current plates are of the 2007 standard (GA36-2007), blue background and consist a one-character provincial abbreviation, a letter of the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") corresponding to a certain city in the province, and five numbers or letters of the alphabet (e.g. 京A-12345, for a vehicle in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") or 粤B-12345 for a vehicle from [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen") in [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") province). The numbers are produced at random, and are computer-generated at the issuing office. (A previous registration plate system, with a green background and the full name of the province in Chinese characters, actually had a sequential numbering order, and the numbering system was eventually beset with corruption).

Yellow plates are issued to motorcycles and large vehicles, such as coaches and buses. Black plates are issued to vehicles belonging to diplomatic missions and foreigners (including [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau")). Vehicles registered in Hong Kong or Macau and permitted to enter China would be required to have a separate black plate from China as Hong Kong and Macau operate their own vehicles registration system. The Chinese plates for these cars followed the pattern of the provincial character for Guangdong (粤), the Latin letter "Z", 4 letters and/or numbers, ending in the abbreviated character for the territory (e.g. 港 for Hong Kong and 澳 for Macau).

For motorcycles, the front plate only included five numbers and rear contained the full information (e.g. for a motorcycle registered in Shanghai as 沪C•12345, the front plate would be "12345" and the rear plate bears the entire set).

Hong Kong number plates follow the British system of colouring, with front white and rear yellow plates. The numbering system is two letters and (up to) four digits, e.g. AB1234. Registration plate numbers starting with "AM" are reserved for government use. The front white and rear yellow background is a reflective material to comply with the BS AU145a standard.

In addition, Hong Kong started a new scheme in 2006 to allow personalised registration plates, with up to eight selectable letters or numbers.

Macau local registration plates follow the [Portuguese](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Portugal "Vehicle registration plates of Portugal") pre-1992 system of colour and sequence. ### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: India")]

Main articles: [Vehicle registration plates of India](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_India "Vehicle registration plates of India") and [List of Regional Transport Office districts in India](/wiki/List_of_Regional_Transport_Office_districts_in_India "List of Regional Transport Office districts in India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Indian_Vehicle_Registration_Plate_-_Kolkata_2011-07-29_4088_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Indian_Vehicle_Registration_Plate_-_Kolkata_2011-07-29_4088_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Indian_Vehicle_Registration_Plate_-_Kolkata_2011-07-29_4088_(cropped).JPG)

Indian vehicle registration plate ([West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal")). WB-06 stands for Kolkata.

Vehicle registration plates, usually known as number plates, are issued by the [Regional Transport Office](/wiki/Regional_Transport_Office "Regional Transport Office") of each district. Most motor vehicles which are used on public roads are required by law to display them.
The new system which is followed currently in all the states and cities came into effect in the early 1990s. The scheme comprises:

* A two letter identification for the state in which the vehicle is registered.
* A two number code to identify the [Regional Transport Office](/wiki/Regional_Transport_Office "Regional Transport Office") (RTO) where the vehicle is registered.
* An alphabet code to define the series. (one or two Alphabet, depending on vehicle density of the district/RTO)
* A four digit serial.

For Example, in the case of "MH 10 EL 5311", "MH" stands for [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra"), "10" stands for [Sangli](/wiki/Sangli "Sangli") city RTO, and "EL 5311" denotes the series and serial number.

The Delhi NCR however uses a modified system wherein an additional alphabet is inserted after the RTO code to classify vehicle type. For example, a Delhi registration plate may read "DL 12 C AB 0496" where "DL" stands for Delhi, "12 C" stands for Car, and "AB 0496" is the series and number. In this scheme, 'C' denotes Car, 'S' denotes Scooter/Motorcycle, 'R' stands for rickshaw (three-wheeler), 'F' stands for "Fancy" or VIP numbers irrespective of vehicle type; and "P" for Public transport vehicles.

Some states have been adapting the dual letter series code system, for example car series' are CA, CB, CC; motorbike series' are MA, MB and so on. Most states however still use the standard series code, denoted by a single letter of the alphabet.

For electric vehicles, the design has additional blue trim at the expiry date row.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/2023_Indonesian_plate_%282-3-wheeler%2C_private%29.svg/250px-2023_Indonesian_plate_%282-3-wheeler%2C_private%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2023_Indonesian_plate_(2-3-wheeler,_private).svg)

For two-or-three wheelers, the design has the same colour scheme, but with a smaller height and width, and has not yet implemented the FE-Schrift typeface.

Indonesian vehicle plates share the legacy of the Dutch colonial era, which do not reflect the regional divisions of the country into [provinces](/wiki/Provinces "Provinces"), but the old system of *karesidenan* or [residencies](/wiki/Residency_(country_subdivision) "Residency (country subdivision)"). Their prefixes are therefore based on this system. There are four main colour schemes (white, red, yellow, green) that are used in Indonesia and consist of a combination of alphabet and numbers.

* For privately owned vehicles, a white background with black typeface (from June 2022, and the newer FE-Schrift font from November 2022). Before that, a black background with white typeface.
* For government-owned vehicles, a red background with white typeface.
* For commercial and public vehicles, a yellow background with black typeface.
* For vehicles exclusively used in [free-trade zones](/wiki/Free_trade_zones "Free trade zones") i.e. [Batam](/wiki/Batam "Batam") (see [Indonesia–Malaysia–Singapore Growth Triangle](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia%E2%80%93Singapore_Growth_Triangle "Indonesia–Malaysia–Singapore Growth Triangle")), a green background with black typeface.
* For electric vehicles, there is additional blue trim at the expiry date row.
* For two-or-three wheelers (motorcycles, autorickshaws, etc.), the design has the same colour scheme but with a smaller height and width, and has not yet implemented the FE-Schrift font. Electric two-or-three wheelers also have blue trim at the expiry date row.
* Dealer plates are white background with red typeface, usually for vehicles yet to have legal and confirmed information and owner.

Besides these normal plates, there are also military plates for Army, Navy, Air Force, and also the Police. While diplomatic corps get special white plates and black numbering with "CD" prefix. The normal scheme comprises a one or two letters identification for the regencies, followed by an up to four digit numbers for the car's identification, and the last one to three letters are the serial code or district identification. The expiry date of the license is embossed along the bottom and some on the top of the plate. At the middle of the plate number, the numbers are usually random or requested by the vehicle owner and has a maximum of four digits and a maximum of three letters at the end of the vehicle's plate number, for example it could be: **(B 1 A)**, **(B 12 AB)**, **(B 123 AB)**, and **(B 1234 ABC)**. Sometimes the last maximum three letters at the end of the plate identifies the district region of the registered vehicle by the first letter, for example: **(B 1234 WIE)** which "**W**" identifies the vehicle is from the region of Southern [South Tangerang](/wiki/South_Tangerang "South Tangerang") city (Kota Tangerang Selatan), Banten province. Vehicle owners may request their vehicle's last letters plate for their own desire, but would need more affairs by the local police registering it, for example that the owner's name is "Adi" then he would make his vehicle's plate number like so: **(B 1234 ADI)**.

Example:

* **B 1234 AB**, is mainly a vehicle registered in [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"), distinguishable from the letter code from the first letter of the plate, "**B**" represents the following cities: [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"), [Depok](/wiki/Depok "Depok"), [Tangerang](/wiki/Tangerang "Tangerang") (includes [South Tangerang](/wiki/South_Tangerang "South Tangerang")), and [Bekasi](/wiki/Bekasi "Bekasi"). Mainly, vehicles registered in 2008 and later starts using three letters at the end of the whole plate, for example: **B 1234 ABC**
* **L 123 MN**, is a vehicle registered in [Surabaya](/wiki/Surabaya "Surabaya"), the provincial capital of East Java Surabaya code is "**L**"
* **DB 787 AA**, is a vehicle registered in [North Sulawesi](/wiki/North_Sulawesi "North Sulawesi") which includes [Minahasa](/wiki/Minahasa "Minahasa") and [Manado](/wiki/Manado "Manado") which is the capital city of the province. North Sulawesi code is "**DB**"
* **KT 8910 T**, is a vehicle registered in [East Kalimantan](/wiki/East_Kalimantan "East Kalimantan") Province, [Tarakan](/wiki/Tarakan "Tarakan") municipality, on Borneo island. East Kalimantan code is "**KT**"
* **F 8888 LU**, is a vehicle registered in [Bogor](/wiki/Bogor "Bogor") city, [West Java](/wiki/West_Java "West Java"). Bogor city code is "**F**"

The plates usually have their expiration dates shown below or very few on the top of the serial numbers, indicating its expiry month and year, so if it says (12•26) it means that the plate expires at December 2026, so the owner of the vehicle should pay tax and get a new plate at that time, to which the process is redone every five years. A new plate design introduced in April 2011 eliminates a white line circling the whole plate and has a thinner typeface until it was replaced in 2022.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Iran_-_Private_Vehicle_License_Plate_Guide.png/250px-Iran_-_Private_Vehicle_License_Plate_Guide.png)](/wiki/File:Iran_-_Private_Vehicle_License_Plate_Guide.png)

Private vehicle license plate guide

Iranian license plates have had European standard dimensions since 2005. Each province in Iran has multiple unique, two-digit codes that are included at the right end of the license plates in a distinguished square outline, above which the word "[ایران](/wiki/Iran "Iran")" or "[Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran")" has been written. A province's license plates will not be issued with a new code unless all possible combinations with the old code have been issued.

In Tehran, the first code to be issued for the province was code 11, and subsequent codes all increased by 11 as well (meaning codes 11, 22, 33, ..., 99 are unique to Tehran.) Ever since code 99 was fully issued, the new codes for Tehran have started from 10 and subsequently increased by 10 (10, 20, 30, ...).

Private vehicles' plates are black on white with a blue strip on the left. The code in the square represents the [regional codes](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Iran#Area_codes "Vehicle registration plates of Iran"). The letter can be dependent on where the car's owner's principal address is located. For example, while regional code "**83"** belongs to [Fars Province](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province"), letter "**م"** (M) is dedicated to residents of [Larestan County](/wiki/Larestan_County "Larestan County"), while letter "**هـ"** (H) is dedicated to [Jahrom County](/wiki/Jahrom_County "Jahrom County") residents.

Iran's license plate format is entirely in [Persian alphabet](/wiki/Persian_alphabet "Persian alphabet"). It follows the format **## X ### - NN**

* **## ###** is the registration code
* **X** is the series letter. Each unique classification of vehicles is assigned a unique letter. For private vehicles, for example, if numbers start from *11 B 111*, the letter *B* will not change until numbers reach **99 B 999**. Then, plates will go up to *11 C111*. These details are explained further in each section of this article.
* **NN** is the province code.

| Private vehicle letter series on Iranian license plates | | | | | | | | | | | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Letter | ب | ج | د | س | ‌ص | ط | ق | ل | م | ن | و | هـ | ی |
| Latin equivalent | B | J | D | S | Ṣ | Ṭ | Q | L | M | N | V | H | Y |

Example:

* **12 V 345-93**, is a vehicle registered in [Shiraz](/wiki/Shiraz "Shiraz"). distinguishable from the last two digits of the plate,"**93**" representing [Fars province](/wiki/Fars_province "Fars province"). And the letter "**V**" representing Shiraz city.
* **12 Q 345-99**, is a vehicle registered in [Tehran](/wiki/Tehran "Tehran"). distinguishable from the last two digits of the plate, "**99**" representing Tehran City.

### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Vehicle_registration_plate&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [Vehicle registration plates of Japan](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Japan "Vehicle registration plates of Japan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Japanese_green_on_white_license_plate.png/250px-Japanese_green_on_white_license_plate.png)](/wiki/File:Japanese_green_on_white_license_plate.png)

[Japanese registration plate](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Japan "Vehicle registration plates of Japan") (schematic illustration). This sample registration plate is registered to [Tama](/wiki/Tama,_Tokyo "Tama, Tokyo").

Japanese vehicle registration plates fall into two classes: Prefectural, used nationwide, and Municipal. Municipal registration is typically applicable to motor vehicles that will not leave the area, such as light motorcycles.

In the prefectural system, the top line names the office at which the vehicle is registered, and includes a numeric code that indicates the class of vehicle. The bottom contains one serial letter (typically a [kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")), and up to four digits. The classes of registration plate are divided by vehicle type and engine size. For private vehicles [less than 660 cc (40 cu in)](/wiki/Kei_car "Kei car"), registration plates have black text on a yellow background. Above 660 cc (40 cu in), a white plate with green text is used. For commercial, non-private vehicles, the colors of the number plate are inverted. An official seal is applied over one (typically the left) screw, preventing the plate being removed and applied to another car.

Municipal registration plates in Japan may vary in color and design.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
